title: Buttermilk cold-process soap
subtitle: A creamy conditioning bar using buttermilk as the water substitute with a high olive oil content
excerpt: Buttermilk replaces all the water in the lye solution, producing a bar with a notably creamy lather. The lactic acid in buttermilk makes the bar feel noticeably softer in use than a plain-water equivalent. Oil base is olive at 70%, coconut at 20%, and shea butter at 10% at 5% superfat.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, shea butter 0.128. 5% superfat applied. Buttermilk as total water substitute in cold-process soap: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997). Dairy soaps and temperature management: Anne Bramson, Soap: Making It, Enjoying It (1972). Lactic acid in cosmetics: EU Cosmetics Regulation (EC) No 1223/2009.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale by running revised oil weights through a lye calculator. Buttermilk volume scales at the same proportion as the water it replaces.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Freeze the buttermilk in an ice-cube tray the night before. Using frozen buttermilk prevents the dairy sugars from scorching when the lye is added.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Cured bars keep two years in a cool dark place. Dairy soaps tend to a slightly deeper cream or tan colour than plain-water bars; this is normal. Discard if bars show orange oily patches or a rancid smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The reaction between fat and lye that produces soap. The SAP value of each oil gives the grams of lye needed to fully convert one gram of that oil.
The percentage of oil remaining unsaponified in the finished bar, contributing a mild, conditioning quality.
The point at which the soap batter emulsifies to a thick custard. Dairy batches often trace faster than plain-water batches.

content.text: Buttermilk replaces all the water in this recipe. It gives the bar a creamy, mild lather because its proteins survive the
process. The key step is freezing the buttermilk first: it stops the dairy sugars from scorching when the lye is added. The shea butter and 5%
add to the conditioning feel of the bar.

items.prepNote: frozen in ice-cube tray overnight; total liquid for lye solution

content.text: Work in a ventilated space with safety goggles and chemical-resistant protective gloves. Remove the frozen buttermilk cubes from the freezer just before making the lye solution.

Place the frozen buttermilk cubes in the HDPE pitcher. Add the sodium hydroxide in a slow, steady stream over the frozen cubes, stirring constantly. Never add water or liquid to lye; always add lye to liquid. The frozen dairy slows the heat rise and keeps the sugars from scorching. The solution will still heat significantly; do not lean over the pitcher. Once the lye is fully dissolved, the mixture will be slushy then liquid. Cool to 38 to 42 degrees C.
Melt the coconut oil and shea butter, combine with the olive oil, and cool to 38 to 42 degrees C.
Pour the lye-buttermilk solution into the oils in a thin stream and stick-blend to light

. Dairy batches can accelerate to medium trace quickly; pour into the mould at light trace without delay. Do not insulate this batch with a towel; dairy soaps generate more heat during the gel phase and overheating can cause a soda-ash crust. Leave the mould covered but not wrapped.

Unmould after 2 to 3 days. The high olive oil content means this bar takes longer to set than a coconut-oil-heavy formula. Cut into bars 2.5 cm thick and place on a rack. Cure for 4 to 6 weeks. The bar's final colour is a warm cream, slightly darker than a plain-water soap.

Store cured bars on an open soap dish. A cool dark cupboard is better than a bathroom shelf for bars waiting to be used. Keeps two years. Discard if the bar develops orange oily patches or a rancid or sour smell.
